youtube.title: Bananas Are Berries, Strawberries Aren't #Shorts
youtube.description:
Bananas are berries. Strawberries aren't. Everything you learned about fruit is backwards.

Plant biology has a strict definition of what counts as a true berry, and it has nothing to do with how we use the word in everyday conversation. A berry is technically a fleshy fruit that develops from a single flower's ovary and contains seeds embedded in the flesh. By this scientific standard, bananas qualify—they grow from one ovary and their tiny black specs are seeds. Strawberries, however, develop from multiple ovaries in a single flower, making them aggregate fruits, not berries at all.

This distinction matters because it reveals how our common language diverges from botanical precision. Raspberries and blackberries aren't technically berries either—they're aggregates of drupelets. Meanwhile, grapes, blueberries, and even tomatoes meet the strict berry criteria. The same applies to bananas, kiwis, and papayas. Understanding this botanical classification changes how we think about produce in the grocery store and highlights why scientists need precise terminology to communicate accurately.

The confusion stems from centuries of casual fruit naming before modern plant science formalized these categories. Today, botanists distinguish between true berries and false berries, a distinction that matters in agriculture, nutrition research, and evolutionary biology. It's a perfect example of how scientific knowledge can completely overturn everyday assumptions.

If plant biology counts, what other 'fruits' do you think aren't actually berries?

instagram.caption:
Bananas are berries, strawberries aren't. Plant biology is wild.

A true berry develops from one flower's ovary with seeds inside the flesh. Bananas fit perfectly. Strawberries? They're aggregate fruits from multiple ovaries—not berries at all. Same with raspberries and blackberries. Meanwhile, grapes and tomatoes ARE technically berries.

If plant biology counts, what other fruits do you think aren't actually berries?
